contest.title: 2024-2025 ICPC Asia Jakarta Regional Contest (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Teams Preferred)

sources.notes: Read the full statement, all samples, and limits R,C <= 1000, odd X <= 9, and Q <= 100000.
Read the official Jakarta analysis for G on page 9; confirmed the unit-face zero-cycle criterion and root-potential query formula.
Read the top HappyGhast solution; independently confirmed antisymmetric edge weights, the elementary-square cycle basis, and path independence.
Re-derived the cycle-space proof and checked unit-face equivalence against spanning-tree potential consistency on 3000 random grids up to 4 by 4.

annotation.summary: Answer minimum-penalty path queries on a digit-height grid with antisymmetric directed edge costs, or report INVALID when penalties are unbounded below.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= R,C <= 1000; heights are digits 0..9; X is odd and 1 <= X <= 9; Q <= 100000. The connected grid and query count require one linear preprocessing pass and O(1) work per query.
annotation.core_idea: For an adjacent move u->v, w(u,v)=(H_u-H_v)^X=-w(v,u) because X is odd. Thus any cycle with nonzero total weight has a negative orientation, while validity is equivalent to every cycle summing to zero. The cycle space of a rectangular grid is generated by its oriented 2-by-2 cell boundaries, so scanning every elementary face is sufficient. If one face sum is nonzero, the connected grid lets every query visit and repeat its negative orientation, hence every answer is INVALID. Otherwise the weights are conservative: assign p(v) as the weight of any path from (1,1) to v. Zero cycle sums make p path-independent, and every query answer is p(target)-p(start). A DFS or any fixed spanning-tree traversal computes all p values; no per-query shortest-path run is needed.
annotation.complexity: O(R*C + Q) time and O(R*C) memory.
tricks: odd X makes opposite directed edge weights negatives
a nonzero cycle yields a negative cycle in one orientation
elementary grid faces generate the whole cycle space
zero circulation turns path costs into vertex-potential differences

solution_variants.name: face-cycle-check-and-vertex-potentials
solution_variants.summary: Check the oriented weight sum around each 2-by-2 face. Reject all queries if any is nonzero; otherwise traverse the grid once to assign root-to-vertex potentials and answer each pair by subtracting potentials.
solution_variants.complexity: O(R*C + Q) time and O(R*C) memory.

tags.evidence: The decisive validity proof is that elementary square boundaries form a cycle basis of the grid, so zero weight on every basis cycle is equivalent to zero weight on every cycle.
After all cycle sums vanish, the accumulated edge weight from the root to a vertex is invariant under the chosen path, enabling a well-defined vertex potential.
The grid embedding makes bounded faces the generators used to certify every cycle, rather than requiring arbitrary cycle enumeration.
The requested minimum path cost collapses to p(target)-p(start) because every path between the same endpoints has equal weight once all cycle sums are zero.
